lines.target: The evolution of the English language over time and the fact that it is spoken by more than 350 million people, have allowed it to free itself from irregularities and difficulties that burden other languages; thus English has become a "simple" language.
lines.base: La evolución de la lengua inglesa durante el tiempo y el hecho de que la hablan más de 350 millones de personas, han permitido que se haya podido deshacer de las irregularidades y dificultades que turbaban otras lenguas; así el inglés se ha convertido en una lengua "simple".

lines.target: But this logic made of simplicity does not depart from reality: between 1926 and 1930, the English writer and linguist Charles Kay Ogden established a variant of English, which he baptized Basic English. This new language, whose vocabulary and to a certain extent grammar are derived from English, contained in its basic version only 850 words (600 nouns, 150 adjectives and 100 function words like can, to, if, etc.). Basic English aspired to be the international auxiliary language and, as such, received the support of Winston Churchill and Franklin Roosevelt in 1943. Fortunately, we can say that this "poor relation" aroused little interest in political and official circles, although when we think sometimes that there are too many words in English, we won't take the path of 1984 in the near future.
lines.base: Pero esa lógica hecha de simplicidad no se aparta de la realidad: entre 1926 y 1930, el escritor y lingüista inglés Charles Kay Ogden estableció una variante del inglés, que bautizó Basic English. Esa nueva lengua, cuyo vocabulario y en parte también la gramática se derivan del inglés, contenía en su versión básica solo 850 palabras (600 nombres, 150 adjetivos y 100 palabras "funcionales" como can, to, if, etc.). Basic English aspiraba a ser la "lengua auxiliar internacional" y, como tal, recibió el apoyo de Winston Churchill y de Franklin Roosevelt en 1943. Afortunadamente, podemos decir que "ese pariente pobre" suscitó poco interés en los medios políticos u oficiales) aunque, cuando a veces se piensa que hay demasiadas palabras en inglés, no tomaremos el camino del 1984 en un futuro probable.

lines.target: A rose is a rose is a rose... is the title of a poem by Gertrude Stein (who lived for a long time in France and who pronounced the phrase in response to someone who told her that a rose was more beautiful than other flowers). It could be translated as: A rose is a rose is a rose.
lines.base: Una rosa es una rosa es una rosa... es el título de un poema de Gertrude Stein (que residió mucho tiempo en Francia y que pronunció la frase en respuesta a alguien que le dijo que la rosa era más bella que las otras flores). Se podría traducir por: Una rosa es una rosa es una rosa.

lines.target: Now, as for the relative pronouns: as we have already said, and as Lesson 45 illustrates, the language has evolved and the subjunctive has been subtly replaced by other turns of phrase, also correct but which do not always please the purists and those responsible for safeguarding linguistic purity (a thankless task in the Anglophone world!).
lines.base: Ahora, en cuanto a los relativos: como ya hemos dicho, y como lo ilustra la Lección 45, la lengua ha evolucionado y el subjuntivo se ha reemplazado sutilmente por otros giros, también correctos pero que no siempre agradan a los puristas y a los encargados de servar la pureza lingüística (¡un trabajo ingrato en el mundo anglófono!).

lines.target: The form of the present subjunctive is the same as the infinitive without "to" (be, go), etc. and it is used only in the third person singular: Long live the king!
lines.base: La forma del subjuntivo presente es la misma que el infinitivo sin "to" (be, go), etc. y se le utiliza solamenta en la tercera persona del singular: ¡Larga vida al rey!

lines.target: The subjunctive is used in set expressions, such as God save the Queen, as it may... (such as it is) or if I were you.
lines.base: El subjuntivo se emplea en las expresiones consagradas, tales como Dios salve a la Reina, as it may... (así son las cosas) o if I were you.

lines.target: It is normally used after verbs like to command, to insist, to order, etc., but as we have seen, it is often replaced by either the infinitive without "to" (which, it must be admitted, is somewhat vicious, because the subjunctive and the infinitive without "to" have exactly the same form!) or by other turns of phrase to avoid the problem altogether: The accountant recommended that the company buy a new machine.
lines.base: Se emplea normalmente después de los verbos como to command, to insist, to order, etc., pero como hemos visto, se substituye a menudo por si el infinitivo sin to (lo que, hay que confesar, es bastante vicioso, ¡porque el subjuntivo tiene exactamente la misma forma!) o por otros giros para evitar el problema completamente: El contador recomendó que la compañía comprara una nueva máquina.

lines.target: So, apart from set expressions, we find little of the subjunctive (and sometimes we don't recognize it when we see it!), and the English unconsciously find ways to avoid it (in a formal style, Americans have less tendency to avoid this mood; see Lesson 46).
lines.base: Así pues, aparte de las expresiones consagradas, encontramos poco el subjuntivo (¡y a veces lo vemos, no lo reconocemos!), y los ingleses encuentran inconscientemente la manera de evitarlo (en un estilo formal, los americanos tienen menos tendencia a evitar ese modo; ver lección 46).

lines.target: In particular, the goal of this lesson is to acquire awareness of the extent of English vocabulary and, consequently, to avoid the temptation of "getting by" with words that all look the same.
lines.base: En particular, la meta de esta lección es la de adquirir conciencia de la extensión del vocabulario inglés y, por consiguiente, la de evitarle la tentación de "desenvolverse" con palabras que se parecen todas.

lines.target: In fact, to get, a lot, and nice are very used words, which people use to avoid looking for a more appropriate word, and everything becomes nice, the ubiquitous adjective that means everything and says nothing. Better than saying: He's a nice man, let us be precise: charming, delightful, entertaining, and generous...
lines.base: De hecho, to get, a lot, y nice son palabras muy utilizadas, que evitan buscar una palabra más apropiada, y todo se convierte en nice, el adjetivo ubicuo que quiere decirlo todo y no dice nada. Mejor que decir: He's a nice man, precisemos: charming, delightful, entertaining, y generous...

lines.target: At the beginning of your learning, simple words have been very useful to you, but now it's time to evolve.
lines.base: Al principio de su aprendizaje, palabras simples le han sido muy útiles, pero ya es la hora de evolucionar.

lines.target: In lesson forty-seven, we saw some very precise words for describing noises and lights. There are many others, which make English a sensual language rather than an intellectual one (and therefore...).
lines.base: En esta lección cuarenta y siete, vemos unas palabras muy precisas para describir los ruidos y las luces. Hay tantas otras, que hacen del inglés una lengua sensual más bien que intelectual (¡y por eso...!).

lines.target: We strongly recommend that you develop your vocabulary by reading and taking note of new words, but always within their context.
lines.base: Le recomendamos con insistencia que desarrolle su vocabulario leyendo y tomando nota de las nuevas palabras, pero siempre dentro de su contexto.

lines.target: The English and American parliamentary systems are similar (for example, both are bicameral), but taking into account that one is a monarchy and the other a republic, the political modalities are somewhat different.
lines.base: Los sistemas parlamentarios inglés y americanos son semejantes (por ejemplo, los dos son bicamerales), pero teniendo en cuenta que se trata de una monarquía y de una república, las modalidades políticas son algo diferentes.

lines.target: In Great Britain, Parliament (note pronunciation: PAH-li-ment) is composed of two chambers: the House of Commons and the House of Lords. The first has 650 deputies (Members of Parliament or MPs) elected by universal suffrage every five years. The House of Lords is composed of about 1200 peers (nobles), who are either hereditary peers (the noble title passes from generation to generation) or life peers (appointed by the monarch, and their title disappears with them). The peers are therefore not elected by the people. The head of government is the Prime Minister; there is no limit to the term of a Prime Minister. Great Britain does not have a written constitution.
lines.base: En Gran Bretaña, el parlamento (Parliament: atención a la pronunciación, PAHIement) está compuesto de dos cámaras: The House of Commons y the House of Lords. La primera cuenta con 650 diputados (Members of Parliament o MPs) elegidos por sufragio universal cada cinco años. La Cámara de los Lores está compuesta de alrededor de 1200 pares (peers), que son ya sea hereditary peers (el título de nobleza pasa de generación a generación) o life peers (son nombrados por el monarca, y su título desaparece con ellos). Los pares no son por lo tanto elegidos por el pueblo. El jefe del gobierno es el Primer Ministro (Prime Minister); no hay límite para el mandato de un Primer Ministro. Gran Bretaña no posee una constitución escrita.

lines.target: From this lesson on, we will mark with an asterisk the words whose spelling differs between British and American English; refer to the appendix on this subject at the end of the book.
lines.base: A partir de esta lección, señalaremos con un asterisco las palabras cuya ortografía difiere entre el inglés de GB y el inglés de EU; refiérase al apéndice sobre esta materia al final del libro.

gap_candidates.target: The evolution of the English language over time and the fact that it is spoken by more than 350 million people, have allowed it to free itself from irregularities and difficulties that burden other languages; thus English has become a "simple" language.

But this logic made of simplicity does not depart from reality: between 1926 and 1930, the English writer and linguist Charles Kay Ogden established a variant of English, which he baptized Basic English. This new language, whose vocabulary and to a certain extent grammar are derived from English, contained in its basic version only 850 words (600 nouns, 150 adjectives and 100 function words like can, to, if, etc.). Basic English aspired to be the international auxiliary language and, as such, received the support of Winston Churchill and Franklin Roosevelt in 1943. Fortunately, we can say that this "poor relation" aroused little interest in political and official circles, although when we think sometimes that there are too many words in English, we won't take the path of 1984 in the near future.

A rose is a rose is a rose... is the title of a poem by Gertrude Stein (who lived for a long time in France and who pronounced the phrase in response to someone who told her that a rose was more beautiful than other flowers). It could be translated as: A rose is a rose is a rose.

Now, as for the relative pronouns: as we have already said, and as Lesson 45 illustrates, the language has evolved and the subjunctive has been subtly replaced by other turns of phrase, also correct but which do not always please the purists and those responsible for safeguarding linguistic purity (a thankless task in the Anglophone world!).
The form of the present subjunctive is the same as the infinitive without "to" (be, go), etc. and it is used only in the third person singular: Long live the king!

It is normally used after verbs like to command, to insist, to order, etc., but as we have seen, it is often replaced by either the infinitive without "to" (which, it must be admitted, is somewhat vicious, because the subjunctive and the infinitive without "to" have exactly the same form!) or by other turns of phrase to avoid the problem altogether: The accountant recommended that the company buy a new machine.

So, apart from set expressions, we find little of the subjunctive (and sometimes we don't recognize it when we see it!), and the English unconsciously find ways to avoid it (in a formal style, Americans have less tendency to avoid this mood; see Lesson 46).
In particular, the goal of this lesson is to acquire awareness of the extent of English vocabulary and, consequently, to avoid the temptation of "getting by" with words that all look the same.

In fact, to get, a lot, and nice are very used words, which people use to avoid looking for a more appropriate word, and everything becomes nice, the ubiquitous adjective that means everything and says nothing. Better than saying: He's a nice man, let us be precise: charming, delightful, entertaining, and generous...

In lesson forty-seven, we saw some very precise words for describing noises and lights. There are many others, which make English a sensual language rather than an intellectual one (and therefore...).
We strongly recommend that you develop your vocabulary by reading and taking note of new words, but always within their context.

The English and American parliamentary systems are similar (for example, both are bicameral), but taking into account that one is a monarchy and the other a republic, the political modalities are somewhat different.

In Great Britain, Parliament (note pronunciation: PAH-li-ment) is composed of two chambers: the House of Commons and the House of Lords. The first has 650 deputies (Members of Parliament or MPs) elected by universal suffrage every five years. The House of Lords is composed of about 1200 peers (nobles), who are either hereditary peers (the noble title passes from generation to generation) or life peers (appointed by the monarch, and their title disappears with them). The peers are therefore not elected by the people. The head of government is the Prime Minister; there is no limit to the term of a Prime Minister. Great Britain does not have a written constitution.
From this lesson on, we will mark with an asterisk the words whose spelling differs between British and American English; refer to the appendix on this subject at the end of the book.
